1.synthesis: Joseph presents five of his brothers to Pharaoh, who asks their occupation and grants them Goshen when they declare themselves shepherds (vv.1–6). Then Joseph brings in his father Jacob. The encounter between the Egyptian sovereign and the aged patriarch inverts the expected power dynamic: Jacob blesses Pharaoh — twice (vv.7, 10). When Pharaoh asks his age, Jacob answers with characteristic theological weight: 'The days of the years of my sojourning are 130 years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life, and they have not attained to the days of the years of the life of my fathers in the days of their sojourning' (v.9). Jacob names his entire life a sojourning — a pilgrim journey — and calls its days 'few and evil,' not a complaint against God but an honest reckoning of the road traveled. He blesses Pharaoh and departs.
1.historical_context: Jacob's blessing of Pharaoh — a Hebrew patriarch blessing the ruler of the most powerful empire of his day — enacts the Abrahamic promise: 'in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed' (Genesis 12:3). The inferior blesses the superior; the patron of all is blessed by the landless nomad. The patriarchal age commonly used 'sojourning' (gûr, to live as a temporary resident alien) as the self-designation of the covenant people in Canaan: Abraham sojourned (12:10; 20:1), Isaac sojourned (26:3), Jacob sojourned (28:4; 47:9). The self-description 'days of my sojourning' is thus a theological statement: Jacob does not consider himself a permanent resident anywhere on earth. Hebrews 11:13 cites this entire patriarchal pattern: 'these all died in faith … having acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth.'
1.literary_structure: The section's formal structure — presentation of brothers, then father; Pharaoh's questions about occupation and age — follows court protocol. But the theological content inverts the social hierarchy: the alien patriarch blesses the reigning sovereign. Jacob's 'few and evil' (v.9) characterization of his life has been read as ingratitude, but commentators note it is accurate: the deception of his father, the twenty years with Laban, Rachel's death, Joseph's loss, Simeon's detention. Jacob does not romanticize his past. The pilgrim self-designation frames the entire patriarchal story as one of journey toward a destination not yet arrived at — and gives the Exodus its typological context: the sojourners will depart Egypt toward the land promised to the sojourning fathers.
1.theological_application: Gill on Jacob's blessing: 'he gave him his paternal and prophetical blessing, as a man of God, or as a prophet, praying for all manner of happiness, temporal and spiritual, to him and his family.' Matthew Henry: 'Jacob, at his coming in, blessed Pharaoh, and at his going out blessed him again … those who are old should be ready to bless.' Gill on 'few and evil': 'he does not say this in a complaining manner, as if he had reason to quarrel with Providence, but in a humble, modest way … he had met with many difficulties, troubles, and afflictions … yet all are to be ascribed to his own sin and folly.' The pilgrim self-understanding — 'stranger and exile on the earth' — is the posture that Hebrews commends as the mark of authentic faith: living toward a city 'whose designer and builder is God' (Hebrews 11:10).
1.christology: The lesser blesses the greater (cf. Hebrews 7:7, where this principle is applied to Melchizedek blessing Abraham) — in Jacob's blessing of Pharaoh, the alien covenant people are the source of blessing for the nations. This is the Abrahamic commission embodied: the people through whom all families of the earth will be blessed stand before the most powerful man of their age and bless him. Jacob's 'sojourning' language gives the letter to the Hebrews its dominant metaphor: the people of God are pilgrims and exiles whose true city has not yet appeared (Hebrews 11:13–16). Christ himself, 'here we have no lasting city' (Hebrews 13:14), is the destination toward which all patriarchal sojourning was aimed.

13.synthesis: The famine is severe in all the earth. Joseph collects first money (v.14–15), then livestock (v.16–17), then land and persons (vv.18–21) for Pharaoh, as the Egyptians exhaust every resource in exchange for grain. By the famine's end, all Egyptian land except the priests' land belongs to Pharaoh, and the population has been moved from city to city (v.21). Joseph then establishes the system by which Egypt will operate going forward: the people will work the land for Pharaoh's benefit, giving one-fifth (20%) to Pharaoh and keeping four-fifths for themselves (vv.23–24). The Egyptians receive this arrangement with relief: 'You have saved our lives' (v.25). The narrator notes at the end that Israel, meanwhile, is prospering in Goshen: 'they gained possessions in it, and were fruitful and multiplied greatly' (v.27).
13.historical_context: The progressive economic consolidation — silver, livestock, land, persons — follows the logic of famine economics in the ancient world: people sell whatever they have to survive. The 20% tax Joseph institutes (one-fifth to Pharaoh, four-fifths to the people) was relatively moderate by ANE standards; Mesopotamian tribute rates could reach 50%. The priestly exemption (v.22) reflects the high status of temple estates in Egyptian political economy — priestly lands were outside royal control. The notice that Joseph 'moved the population to the cities' (v.21) has been read as administrative consolidation: moving people where grain distribution is efficient. The contrast between Egypt's dispossession and Israel's multiplication in Goshen (v.27) is a pointed theological note: while the Egyptians lose everything to the crown, the covenant family gains and multiplies.
13.literary_structure: The famine-economics section is often read as a digression from the Joseph-family narrative, but it serves a structural purpose: it establishes Egypt as a society of total royal dependency (everyone is Pharaoh's servant) precisely as Israel is multiplying in Goshen in a different mode. The contrast is loaded with later meaning: in Exodus, the Israelites will also become 'Pharaoh's servants' — but by coercion, not by exchange, and without the four-fifths retained for themselves. The legal formula 'we will be servants to Pharaoh' (v.25) that the Egyptians speak voluntarily will be imposed on Israel involuntarily in Exodus 1. The legal precedent Joseph establishes — 'Joseph made it a statute … that Pharaoh should have the fifth' (v.26) — is the law under which the later oppression of Israel will be rationalized.
13.theological_application: Gill on the Egyptians' 'you have saved our lives': 'which was literally true; for by his prudence in laying up corn in the seven years of plenty, their lives were preserved in the seven years of famine; and this is applied by some to Christ, the antitype of Joseph, who has saved the lives, the spiritual lives of his people, from death, sin, Satan, and hell.' Matthew Henry: 'Those who have been thus saved from the worst of famines, the famine of the word and of the soul, cannot but say, Thou hast saved our lives; let us be Thy servants.' The Egyptians become Pharaoh's servants by necessity; believers become Christ's servants by gratitude for the salvation received. The contrast between the two modes of servitude — coerced and voluntary — is the NT's recurring theme in the paradox of being 'slaves of righteousness' (Romans 6:18).
13.christology: 'You have saved our lives' (v.25) — the Egyptians credit Joseph with their survival and offer themselves as his servants. Joseph's role as the world's food-giver anticipates Christ as the 'bread of life' who gives himself so that those who come to him will never hunger or thirst (John 6:35). The one whom his brothers rejected and sold into slavery becomes the source of life for all nations. Paul's 'my God will supply every need of yours' (Philippians 4:19) operates on the same theological logic: the God who stored grace for seven years of abundance dispenses it through the seven years of need. Joseph is the one mediator through whom all access to the granary of Egypt must pass — a typology the NT explicitly applies to Christ as the one mediator between God and humanity (1 Timothy 2:5).

27.synthesis: Israel settles in the land of Goshen and — in a pointed contrast to the famine-ravaged Egyptians — 'gained possessions in it, and were fruitful and multiplied greatly' (v.27). Jacob lives seventeen more years in Egypt, reaching 147. As death approaches, he summons Joseph and extracts an oath: 'Do not bury me in Egypt, but let me lie with my fathers. Carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their burial place' (vv.29–30). Joseph swears; Jacob bows in worship at the head of his bed (v.31). The burial-in-Canaan insistence is not mere sentimentality but a covenant act: the body buried in the promised land is a physical pledge that the promise will be fulfilled. The patriarchs die in faith in what has not yet been received; their bones in the land are their testimony.
27.historical_context: Jacob's request for burial in Canaan rather than Egypt follows the pattern established by Abraham (the Machpelah cave, ch.23) and anticipated for Isaac (buried at Machpelah, 35:29). The oath Joseph swears — hand under thigh, the same gesture Abraham's servant used (24:2) — is the most solemn form of patriarchal oath, invoking the covenant that runs through the family line. The seventeen years Jacob lives in Egypt (v.28) precisely mirrors the seventeen years Joseph spent with Jacob before being sold (37:2 — 'Joseph, being seventeen years of age'). The symmetry is almost certainly deliberate: the seventeen years of joy stolen are restored as seventeen years of reunion. Jacob's bowing 'in worship at the head of his bed' (v.31) is his final act of faith — the dying patriarch worships the God who has kept him.
27.literary_structure: The brief section serves as the transitional epilogue to Jacob's settlement in Egypt and the prologue to his death-blessing sequence (chs.48–49). The burial-instruction to Joseph (vv.29–30) will be repeated as a formal charge to all his sons in 49:29–32, creating a narrative bracket around the blessings. Jacob's insistence on Canaan burial connects the death-and-burial sequence to the covenant promise: the land promised to Abraham, to Isaac, to Jacob is the land where the patriarchs' bones will await the resurrection. The bowing in worship (v.31) is the last act of Jacob's faith explicitly mentioned before his death-blessings — faith expressed in worship is his final waking posture.
27.theological_application: Gill on the burial oath: 'this he desired, not merely out of any natural affection to his native country, or from any particular fondness of being buried in the place where his fathers were, or from a desire that his body might not be embalmed after the manner of the Egyptians; but because of the promises of God concerning the land of Canaan being given to his seed, and as an expression of faith in them.' Matthew Henry: 'The dying Jacob worshipped God upon the bed's head — adored the divine wisdom which had brought about the thing he desired so much; gave God thanks for Joseph's promise and his own comfort.' The deathbed worship of v.31 models what Hebrews 11:13 describes: 'these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar.'
27.christology: Jacob's bones-in-Canaan demand is an act of resurrection faith: the body laid in the land of promise testifies that the promise will be fulfilled and the dead will rise to possess it. Hebrews 11:22 cites Joseph's later bones-oath (50:25) as the faith-act at his death: 'by faith Joseph, at the end of his life, made mention of the exodus of the Israelites and gave directions concerning his bones.' The patriarchal bones in Canaan are pledges of the coming Exodus — and the coming resurrection. Jacob, bowing in worship at the head of his bed, joins the cloud of witnesses who testify to what they have not yet received: the city 'whose designer and builder is God' (Hebrews 11:10), to which the covenant people travel through all their sojourning.
